SOURCE 3: SOPHIA - 10-YEAR CORPORATE CAREER TO CAREER BREAK
Rating: 8.5/10
URL: https://medium.com/psychology-of-workplaces/why-i-quit-my-job-after-10-years-c5e0b581acc7
Source Type: Medium Personal Essay / Psychology of Workplaces
Date: October 3, 2024
Author: Sophia

===== STORY ARC =====
Entry-level position → Climbed corporate ladder over 10+ years → Regional office head with small team → Handled everything: events, marketing, member services → Every year: job scope grew, KPIs increased → Always met/crushed targets → Glowing reviews, enviable bonuses → On paper: perfect → Reality: completely burnt out → Leading "one-person band" → Team not aligned, one teammate mastered "dodging work while aiming for maximum recognition" → 2020-2022 pandemic: pivoted fast, virtual events, remote work adapting, scrambled to stay afloat → Pulled through chaos → Things returned to "normal" but something shifted internally → Spark gone, every project felt like item on never-ending to-do list → Morning question: "Do I really have to go in today?" → Realized: devoted so much time/energy to job that family took backseat → Missed birthdays, special moments, times when son was sick → Conference crisis: couldn't be there when son really needed her → "Huge wake-up call" → Final straw: head office assigned new task (manage in-person conferences in other location) on top of already packed job, no extra help/resources, suggested better solution but ignored → Started planning exit → Weighed pros/cons, ensured savings could survive → Picked perfect last day considering office schedules → Nine months of overthinking, second-guessing, wondering if huge mistake → Submitted resignation → "It felt liberating... almost" → Had to endure 3 months notice + another 3 months (boss requested to stay longer for her to fill position) → Finally: goodbye after 10+ years → Stepped away, chose to take break and embrace new chapter

===== KEY EMOTIONAL MOMENTS =====
1. **The emotional rollercoaster**: "The whole experience felt like an emotional rollercoaster. One day, I was ready to toss my office key and ride off into the sunset. Next, I was panicking about the big unknown. You know, the classic 'what if I just made the worst decision of my life?' kind of panic."

2. **Nine-month deliberation**: "It took me nine months of overthinking, second-guessing, and wondering if I was making a huge mistake before I finally hit send on that email."

3. **Perfect on paper**: "On paper, it looked perfect. I was getting glowing reviews and bonuses that most people would envy. But here's the kicker: I was completely burnt out."

4. **One-person band**: "Despite all the success, I felt like I was leading a one-person band. My team? Let's just say not everyone was in tune. One particular teammate seemed to master the art of dodging work while aiming for maximum recognition — talk about a juggling act!"

5. **Teamwork belief vs. reality**: "Now, don't get me wrong, I believe in teamwork. There's nothing better than working with a group of people who are aligned, supportive, and there for each other through thick and thin. But when you're managing KPIs while also managing other people's problems? Well, the thrill starts to fade."

6. **Pandemic pivot**: "Then came the pandemic. From 2020 to 2022, we had to pivot — fast. Suddenly, we were hosting events virtually, adapting to remote work, and scrambling to stay afloat. I went from planning large conferences to managing webinars and virtual seminars, all while keeping my team together in a digital world. Despite the chaos, we pulled through."

7. **Internal shift**: "But when things slowly got back to 'normal,' I realized that something had shifted inside me."

8. **Lost spark**: "I was sad to leave. I had climbed the corporate ladder from entry-level all the way to leading a regional office. The company had given me so much in terms of growth and opportunity, and for that, I'll always be grateful. But the spark I once had? Gone. Every project felt like just another item on a never-ending to-do list. Each morning, I'd ask myself, 'Do I really have to go in today?'"

9. **Family backseat**: "It hit me one day — I had devoted so much time and energy to my job that my family had taken a backseat. I had missed birthdays, special moments, even times when my son was sick."

10. **Conference crisis - huge wake-up call**: "Once, I was so tied up running a conference that I couldn't be there when my son really needed me. That moment was a huge wake-up call."

11. **Final straw**: "The head office handed me a new assignment: manage their in-person conferences in other location — on top of my already packed job. No extra help, no extra resources. I even suggested a better solution for the company, but they ignored it. That's when I knew it was time to move on."

12. **Planning the exit**: "So, I started planning my exit. I weighed the pros and cons, made sure I could survive on savings for a while, and picked the perfect last day, taking into account office schedules and activities."

13. **Liberation with asterisk**: "Then, with a deep breath, I submitted my resignation. It felt liberating… almost. After all, I had to endure three more months of notice (and another three months because my boss requested me to stay a little longer for her to fill up my position)."

14. **Six-month goodbye**: "But finally, the day came. I said goodbye to the company I had called home for more than 10 years."

15. **Mixed transition**: "As I stepped away from employment, I chose to take a break and embrace this new chapter. The transition has been a mixed bag — some days I feel a bit lost, lonely, or like I'm missing out. But other days, I feel a sense of relief, grateful for the time I now have to fully focus on myself and my family."

16. **Optimistic uncertainty**: "It's a journey of adjustment, and while I'm still finding my footing, I'm optimistic that with a new routine will come a deeper sense of fulfillment."

===== ESL MULTIPLIERS =====
- Building New Life: 10-year career to intentional break/reset
- Belonging: From isolated "one-person band" to family focus
- Connection Across Differences: Navigating team dysfunction, pandemic adaptation
- Overcoming "Not Good Enough": Success metrics (glowing reviews, bonuses) vs. internal emptiness
- Persistence: 6-month notice period (!), 9 months of deliberation before quitting
- First-Time Courage: Leaving after decade despite uncertainty
- Identity Crisis: Work consumed identity, family "took backseat"

===== FULL ARTICLE TEXT =====

Why I Quit My Job After 10 Years
The Emotional Rollercoaster Ride
Sophia
4 min read
Oct 3, 2024

Image shows an office lady sending resignation letter and pack stuff
Image by charliepix from Canva

After more than a decade at the same company, I finally did it — I quit! Yep, I handed in my resignation and hit pause on the 9-to-5 grind. But trust me, it wasn't a snap decision. It took me nine months of overthinking, second-guessing, and wondering if I was making a huge mistake before I finally hit send on that email.

The whole experience felt like an emotional rollercoaster. One day, I was ready to toss my office key and ride off into the sunset. Next, I was panicking about the big unknown. You know, the classic "what if I just made the worst decision of my life?" kind of panic.

So, why did I quit?

Well, I was heading up a regional office with a small team. We handled everything: events, marketing, member services — you name it. Every year, the job scope grew, and so did the KPIs. We always meet our targets (sometimes even crush them). On paper, it looked perfect.

I was getting glowing reviews and bonuses that most people would envy. But here's the kicker: I was completely burnt out. Despite all the success, I felt like I was leading a one-person band. My team? Let's just say not everyone was in tune. One particular teammate seemed to master the art of dodging work while aiming for maximum recognition — talk about a juggling act!

Now, don't get me wrong, I believe in teamwork. There's nothing better than working with a group of people who are aligned, supportive, and there for each other through thick and thin. But when you're managing KPIs while also managing other people's problems? Well, the thrill starts to fade.

Cue Ranbir Kapoor: "I believe that working with good people matters because then the work environment is good. If there is a sense of respect and belief among the people you work with, that is when good work is done."

Couldn't have said it better myself.

Then came the pandemic. From 2020 to 2022, we had to pivot — fast. Suddenly, we were hosting events virtually, adapting to remote work, and scrambling to stay afloat. I went from planning large conferences to managing webinars and virtual seminars, all while keeping my team together in a digital world. Despite the chaos, we pulled through. But when things slowly got back to "normal," I realized that something had shifted inside me.

I was sad to leave. I had climbed the corporate ladder from entry-level all the way to leading a regional office. The company had given me so much in terms of growth and opportunity, and for that, I'll always be grateful. But the spark I once had? Gone. Every project felt like just another item on a never-ending to-do list. Each morning, I'd ask myself, "Do I really have to go in today?"

It hit me one day — I had devoted so much time and energy to my job that my family had taken a backseat. I had missed birthdays, special moments, even times when my son was sick. Once, I was so tied up running a conference that I couldn't be there when my son really needed me. That moment was a huge wake-up call.

A ball of straws, symbolizing the final burden
Image by NoName_13 from Pixabay

The final straw?

The head office handed me a new assignment: manage their in-person conferences in other location — on top of my already packed job. No extra help, no extra resources. I even suggested a better solution for the company, but they ignored it. That's when I knew it was time to move on.

So, I started planning my exit. I weighed the pros and cons, made sure I could survive on savings for a while, and picked the perfect last day, taking into account office schedules and activities. Then, with a deep breath, I submitted my resignation. It felt liberating… almost. After all, I had to endure three more months of notice (and another three months because my boss requested me to stay a little longer for her to fill up my position). But finally, the day came. I said goodbye to the company I had called home for more than 10 years.

As I stepped away from employment, I chose to take a break and embrace this new chapter. The transition has been a mixed bag — some days I feel a bit lost, lonely, or like I'm missing out. But other days, I feel a sense of relief, grateful for the time I now have to fully focus on myself and my family. It's a journey of adjustment, and while I'm still finding my footing, I'm optimistic that with a new routine will come a deeper sense of fulfillment.

===== KEY QUOTES =====
"It took me nine months of overthinking, second-guessing, and wondering if I was making a huge mistake before I finally hit send on that email."

"The whole experience felt like an emotional rollercoaster... 'what if I just made the worst decision of my life?' kind of panic."

"On paper, it looked perfect. I was getting glowing reviews and bonuses that most people would envy. But here's the kicker: I was completely burnt out."

"I felt like I was leading a one-person band."

"One particular teammate seemed to master the art of dodging work while aiming for maximum recognition."

"But when you're managing KPIs while also managing other people's problems? Well, the thrill starts to fade."

"But when things slowly got back to 'normal,' I realized that something had shifted inside me."

"The spark I once had? Gone. Every project felt like just another item on a never-ending to-do list."

"Each morning, I'd ask myself, 'Do I really have to go in today?'"

"It hit me one day — I had devoted so much time and energy to my job that my family had taken a backseat."

"Once, I was so tied up running a conference that I couldn't be there when my son really needed me. That moment was a huge wake-up call."

"No extra help, no extra resources. I even suggested a better solution for the company, but they ignored it. That's when I knew it was time to move on."

"It felt liberating… almost."

"I had to endure three more months of notice (and another three months because my boss requested me to stay a little longer for her to fill up my position)."

"I said goodbye to the company I had called home for more than 10 years."

"Some days I feel a bit lost, lonely, or like I'm missing out. But other days, I feel a sense of relief."

===== ANALYSIS =====
This source delivers strong emotional depth through Sophia's decade-long journey and internal conflict. The nine-month deliberation period shows authentic struggle, not impulsive decision. "What if I just made the worst decision of my life?" captures universal fear of career change. The contrast between external success (glowing reviews, bonuses) and internal burnout ("completely burnt out", "spark gone") is powerful. "Leading a one-person band" metaphor effectively conveys isolation despite team leadership role. Team dysfunction detail (coworker dodging work for recognition) adds frustration layer. Pandemic pivot section shows resilience but also breaking point - pulled through chaos but "something shifted inside me." The family wake-up call is visceral and specific: missing son when sick during conference = "huge wake-up call." This parallels Jason's family disconnection. The 6-month notice period (3 months + 3 more months) is remarkable detail showing commitment even while leaving. "It felt liberating... almost" captures ambivalence beautifully. Mixed transition feelings ("lost, lonely" some days, "relief, grateful" others) show honest post-quit reality, not fairy tale. Strong ESL resonance: building new life (decade career to reset), overcoming "not good enough" (success metrics vs. fulfillment), persistence (9 months deliberation + 6 months notice), first-time courage (leaving security after 10 years), identity crisis (work consumed life, family backseat), belonging shift (isolated leadership to family focus). The optimistic uncertainty at end ("still finding my footing") feels authentic rather than resolved. Slightly less visceral detail than Jason's hour of pacing but decade timeline and family crisis add weight.
